title: Book cloth, making your own from cotton fabric
subtitle: How to back plain woven cotton with Japanese tissue and PVA
excerpt: Shop-bought book cloth costs more than the fabric and tissue combined. Making your own from calico or cotton broadcloth takes about 30 minutes and produces cloth with better colour control than commercial equivalents.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), public domain, chapter on covering materials; and from general hand-bookbinding practice documented across multiple public-domain Arts and Crafts era manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: Woven fabric backed with a paper or starch barrier that prevents adhesive from bleeding through to the surface. Used to cover case-bound boards.
Very thin, strong tissue made from kozo or gampi fibre. Used as the backing layer in home-made book cloth to seal the weave against adhesive strike-through.

content.text: is cotton or linen fabric bonded to a starch-paper backing. The backing does two things: it stops PVA bleeding through the weave and onto the visible face, and it gives the cloth enough body to handle cleanly during covering. You can make the same material at home using plain calico, tightly woven cotton broadcloth, or linen and a layer of

The finished cloth is slightly stiffer than the raw fabric but still flexible enough to turn around board corners. Once dry, it stores flat indefinitely. Make it in advance of a binding session rather than the same day so it is fully cured before you start cutting.

items.name: Plain woven cotton or linen fabric
items.description: Calico, cotton broadcloth, or lightweight linen. Pre-wash to remove any dressing. Dry and press flat before use. Colour may be bleached, dyed, or natural.

items.name: Japanese tissue (kozo, 10-15 gsm)
items.description: The thinner the better. Heavier tissue adds too much bulk at spine hinges. Standard conservation tissue sold in A3 sheets works well.

items.name: PVA, acid-free (slug: pva-acid-free)
items.description: Diluted 3:1 (three parts PVA to one part water) for the backing coat.

items.name: Paste brush, 50 mm (slug: paste-brush-bookbinding)
items.description: For spreading the diluted PVA evenly across the fabric.

items.name: Two sheets of plain newsprint or scrap paper
items.description: One to work on, one to weight the cloth while it dries.

content.text: Cut the fabric slightly larger than you need, at least 20 mm oversized on every edge. You will trim it once dry. Lay it wrong-side up on a sheet of scrap paper on a flat surface.

Mix the PVA to a 3:1 ratio with water. The mixture should run off the brush in a thin stream rather than dripping in blobs. Too thick and it will not penetrate the weave; too thin and the cloth will buckle badly as it dries.

Brush the diluted PVA across the wrong side of the fabric in even strokes, working from the centre outward. The fabric will immediately start to contract and pucker as the moisture hits it. Work quickly to get an even coat before it starts to set.

Lay the Japanese tissue onto the wet surface. Place one edge first, then lower it across the rest in one smooth movement rather than pressing down all at once. Bubbles trapped under tissue tear when you try to smooth them out after the PVA begins to grip.

Use the bone folder to smooth from the centre outward with light pressure. The tissue should lie flat with no lifting edges. Any bubbles still visible at this stage will flatten as the cloth dries under weight.

Slide the whole assembly, still on the scrap paper, onto a clean dry surface. Lay a sheet of newsprint on top and weight it with several heavy books. The weight prevents the cloth from curling as the PVA dries and contracts. Leave for 2 hours minimum.

Once dry, peel the cloth away from the scrap paper. Trim to size with the craft knife and metal ruler. The tissue side is now the back; the fabric face is the cover surface. Store flat in a folder until needed.

Cloth with a looser weave (linen scrim, open-weave cotton) needs two tissue layers bonded with separate PVA coats, drying between each. Dyed cloth bleeds if the fabric is not colourfast; pre-wash is essential. For a cloth that accepts blind-tooling or gold foil, brush a thin coat of gum sandarac over the finished face before use.

items.symptom: PVA bleeds through to the fabric face
items.cause: PVA too thin, or fabric weave too open
items.fix: Use a second tissue layer. Apply the first coat, let dry fully, then apply a second tissue sheet with a second coat of PVA.

items.cause: Weighted too lightly or removed from weight too soon
items.fix: Re-dampen the back lightly with a damp cloth, re-flatten, and weight under heavy books for another hour.

items.cause: PVA already starting to grip when tissue was placed
items.fix: Work faster on the PVA coat. Alternatively, use methyl cellulose instead of PVA: it sets more slowly and gives more time to position the tissue.
